mission.title: Install and Configure Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)
mission.description: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the deployment, management, and operations of Kubernetes clusters in Azure. It is ideal for users who want to leverage Kubernetes without the overhead of managing the underlying infrastructure. Key trade-offs include reliance on Azure for management and potential costs associated with resource usage.

steps.title: Install Azure CLI
steps.description:
The Azure CLI is required to interact with Azure services, including AKS.

```bash

curl -sL https://aka.ms/InstallAzureCLIDeb | sudo bash

```

> This command installs the Azure CLI on Debian-based systems.

steps.title: Create AKS Cluster
steps.description:
This command creates a new AKS cluster with the specified version, node count, and region.

```bash

az aks create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --node-count 3 --kubernetes-version 1.31 --location eastus

```

> Replace 'myResourceGroup' and 'myAKSCluster' with your desired names.
commands: az aks create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --node-count 3 --kubernetes-version 1.31 --location eastus

steps.title: Configure kubectl
steps.description:
This command configures kubectl to use the new AKS cluster by updating the kubeconfig file.

```bash

az aks get-credentials --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster

```

> This command merges the AKS cluster credentials into your kubeconfig.

steps.title: Verify Cluster Nodes
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the nodes in the AKS cluster to ensure they are ready.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see the nodes listed with a status of 'Ready'.

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
This step includes setting up monitoring and autoscaling for your AKS cluster.

```bash

az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

az aks update --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --enable-cluster-autoscaler --min-count 1 --max-count 5

```

> Enables monitoring for the AKS cluster.
> Enables cluster autoscaler with specified min and max node counts.
commands: az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

uninstall.title: Delete AKS Cluster
uninstall.description:
This command deletes the AKS cluster and all associated resources, which may result in data loss.

```bash

az aks delete --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --yes --no-wait

```

> This will delete the cluster and all associated resources.

uninstall.title: Remove Azure CLI
uninstall.description:
This command removes the Azure CLI from your system.

```bash

sudo apt-get remove azure-cli

```

> This will uninstall the Azure CLI.

upgrade.title: Upgrade AKS Cluster
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup your workloads and configurations. This command upgrades the AKS cluster to a new version.

```bash

az aks upgrade --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --kubernetes-version 1.31

kubectl get nodes

```

> This upgrades the AKS cluster to version 1.31.
> Verify that all nodes are in 'Ready' state after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to get AKS credentials'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the specified resource group or cluster name is incorrect.

**Fix:**
Verify the resource group and cluster name with 'az aks list'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient quota for requested resources'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that your Azure subscription has reached its resource quota.

**Fix:**
Check your Azure subscription quotas and request an increase if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Nodes are in 'NotReady' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen due to insufficient resources or misconfiguration.

**Fix:**
Check the node conditions with 'kubectl describe nodes' and ensure there are enough resources available.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'kubectl command not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** kubectl is not installed or not in your PATH.

**Fix:**
Install kubectl using 'az aks install-cli'.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new Azure regions, improved integration with Azure Monitor.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 1.30, ensure all workloads are compatible with the new version.

resolution.summary: You should see a list of nodes with 'Ready' status when running 'kubectl get nodes'.
codeSnippets: az aks create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --node-count 3 --kubernetes-version 1.31 --location eastus
az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the Azure CLI and kubectl installed before proceeding.
